walkthrough.title: Unfold the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Ticket to Ride: Nordic Countries set up. First, unfold the beautiful map board and place it in the center of the table. This board shows the Nordic countries of Denmark, Finland, Norway, and Sweden, connected by a network of train routes. Make sure everyone can comfortably reach the board.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Color
walkthrough.text: Each player picks one of the three available colors. Take all forty train car pieces in your chosen color and set them in front of you. You will place these little trains on the board when you claim routes during the game. Also grab the matching scoring marker and place it on the zero space of the scoring track around the board's edge.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Train Car Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the deck of train car cards. These are the large cards showing colored train cars in eight different colors, plus locomotive cards that are wild. Give them a thorough shuffle. Then deal five face-up cards in a row next to the board where everyone can see them. Place the remaining deck face down as the draw pile.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Train Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal four train car cards face down to each player from the draw pile. Go ahead and look at your cards but keep them hidden from other players. These are your starting hand, and you will use them to claim routes on the board.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: Now find the destination ticket cards. These are the smaller cards showing two cities connected by a dotted line and a point value. Shuffle them thoroughly and deal three to each player. Look at your tickets carefully. You must keep at least two of the three, but you may keep all three if you like. Return any you do not want to the bottom of the destination ticket deck.

walkthrough.title: Place the Globetrotter Card
walkthrough.text: Find the Globetrotter bonus card and place it face up beside the board where everyone can see it. This card awards ten bonus points at the end of the game to the player who completes the most destination tickets. It is a nice incentive to take on extra tickets during the game.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The most experienced traveler goes first, or pick a random starting player. Play will proceed clockwise around the table. You are all set up and ready to build train routes across Scandinavia. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Ticket to Ride: Nordic Countries is a standalone two to three player game set across Scandinavia. Your goal is to score the most points by claiming train routes on the map, completing destination tickets that connect two cities, and earning the Globetrotter bonus. The map is tighter than the original Ticket to Ride, which makes blocking and competition more intense.

walkthrough.title: Your Three Action Choices
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must do exactly one of three actions. You can draw train car cards to build up your hand. You can claim a route by playing matching cards to place your trains on the board. Or you can draw more destination tickets for additional scoring opportunities. Let's walk through each action in detail.

walkthrough.title: Action One: Draw Train Car Cards
walkthrough.text: When you draw train car cards, you take two cards total. You can pick from the five face-up cards or draw blind from the top of the deck, in any combination. There is one important exception: if you take a face-up locomotive card, it counts as both of your draws for the turn. Locomotives are powerful because they act as wild cards, but picking one face-up costs your entire draw action. Drawing a locomotive blind from the deck is fine and still lets you draw a second card.

walkthrough.title: Action Two: Claim a Route
walkthrough.text: To claim a route, play a set of train car cards matching the color and length of the route you want. For example, a three-space blue route requires three blue cards. Gray routes can be claimed with any single color. Place your train pieces on every space of the route and immediately score points based on the route length. Longer routes score significantly more points.

walkthrough.title: Route Scoring
walkthrough.text: Routes score points based on their length. A one-car route scores one point. Two cars score two points. Three cars score four points. Four cars score seven points. Five cars score ten points. Six cars score fifteen points. And the massive nine-car route across the north scores a whopping twenty-seven points. The longer the route, the more efficient it is per car spent.

walkthrough.title: Tunnels
walkthrough.text: Some routes on the Nordic map are marked with a special border indicating a tunnel. Tunnels add an element of risk. After you play your cards to claim a tunnel, flip three cards from the top of the draw pile. For each card that matches the color you played, or for each locomotive revealed, you must play one additional matching card. If you cannot or choose not to pay the extra cost, you take all your cards back and your turn is wasted. Tunnels can be tricky, so always keep extra cards in reserve.

walkthrough.title: Ferries
walkthrough.text: Ferry routes are marked with locomotive icons on some of their spaces. Those specific spaces must be filled with locomotive cards. You cannot substitute regular colored cards for the locomotive spaces on a ferry. Locomotives are essential for crossing the water, so make sure you collect enough of them if your destination tickets require ferry routes.

walkthrough.title: Action Three: Draw Destination Tickets
walkthrough.text: You must keep at least one, but you may keep two or all three. Each ticket shows two cities and a point value. If you complete the connection between those cities using your claimed routes by game end, you score the printed points. If you fail, you lose that many points. Choose wisely.

walkthrough.title: Double Routes
walkthrough.text: Some city pairs on the map have two parallel routes between them. In a two-player game, both routes may be claimed by different players or even the same player. In a three-player game, once one route of a double is claimed, the parallel route is also available. This is different from some other Ticket to Ride versions where double routes get restricted at lower player counts.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game end is triggered when any player has two or fewer train cars remaining in their supply at the end of their turn. Every player, including the player who triggered the end, gets one final turn. Then the game proceeds to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: This has three parts. First, add up all the route points you scored during the game, which are already on the scoring track. Second, reveal your destination tickets. Add the point value of each completed ticket and subtract the value of any incomplete tickets. Third, check the Globetrotter bonus. The player who completed the most destination tickets receives ten bonus points. If there is a tie for most tickets, all tied players receive the bonus. Highest total score wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 2-3 player standalone Ticket to Ride set in Scandinavia
Score points via routes, completed tickets, and Globetrotter bonus
Tighter map → more blocking and competition

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Nordic Rails
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three players. Player one, Player two, and Player three will each take different approaches so you can see all the key actions in play. Follow along even if you only have two players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Review Your Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: Everyone should have four train car cards and two or three destination tickets. Take a moment to look at your tickets. Find the cities on the map and trace possible routes between them. Do any of your tickets share common cities or overlapping paths? Tickets that share routes are very efficient because one set of trains can complete multiple goals.

walkthrough.title: Player One's Turn: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: Player one looks at the five face-up cards and spots two blue cards. Since several of their ticket routes need blue segments, they take both blue cards from the display. The display is immediately refilled after each card is taken. Drawing cards that match your planned routes is the bread and butter of the early game.

walkthrough.title: Player Two's Turn: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: Player two needs to cross some water, so ferries are essential. They spot a locomotive in the face-up display and grab it. Remember, taking a face-up locomotive uses your entire draw action, so Player two only gets that one card this turn. Locomotives are powerful but costly to acquire face-up.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Turn: Claiming a Route
walkthrough.text: Player three already has enough matching cards in hand to claim a short two-car route between two cities on one of their tickets. They play two matching colored cards, place two of their train pieces on the route, and immediately score two points. Claiming routes early locks in points and secures key connections before opponents can block them.

walkthrough.title: Why Claiming Early Can Be Smart
walkthrough.text: Player three made a good move by claiming early. On this tight Nordic map, critical routes can get snatched by opponents. If a route you need gets blocked, you may have to take a much longer detour that uses more of your precious forty trains. Securing chokepoints early is often better than hoarding cards for one big play.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Attempting a Tunnel
walkthrough.text: They play three green cards for a three-car tunnel. Now comes the risky part. Three cards are flipped from the draw pile. One of them is green, so Player one must pay one additional green card. Luckily they have one more green card in hand, so they pay it and successfully claim the tunnel route.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Not Keeping Tunnel Reserves
walkthrough.text: What if Player one did not have that extra green card? They would have had to take all their cards back and waste their entire turn. This is the most common tunnel mistake new players make. When planning a tunnel attempt, always keep one or two extra cards of the same color in reserve. Better safe than sorry.

walkthrough.title: Recovery: Building a Buffer
walkthrough.text: If you fail a tunnel attempt, do not panic. Spend your next turn or two drawing more cards of the color you need. Build up a buffer of two or three extra cards beyond the route length before trying again. The tunnel tax is random, but having a cushion makes success far more likely.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Claiming a Ferry Route
walkthrough.text: This ferry has two regular spaces and one locomotive space. They play two orange cards for the regular spaces and one locomotive for the ferry space. Trains go on the board, points are scored. Ferry routes are essential for connecting cities across the water.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring Locomotives
walkthrough.text: Player three has been collecting only colored cards and ignoring locomotives entirely. Now they realize one of their key ticket routes requires a ferry with two locomotive spaces, and they have zero locomotives. They will need to spend several turns collecting locomotives before they can claim that route. Meanwhile, other players might claim routes they need.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Collect Locomotives Steadily
walkthrough.text: Locomotives are wild cards that work for any color and are required for ferries. Drawing one from the deck every few turns builds a flexible reserve without slowing down your color collection too much.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Drawing New Tickets
walkthrough.text: Player one has completed one of their destination tickets and decides to draw more. They take three new destination tickets, look at them, and decide to keep two that share an overlapping path. They return the third to the bottom of the deck. More completed tickets mean more points and a better shot at the Globetrotter bonus.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Taking Too Many Tickets
walkthrough.text: Player three gets excited and draws three new tickets, keeping all three. The problem is that two of those tickets require routes on opposite sides of the map, and with only forty train pieces total, there simply are not enough trains to complete everything. Unfulfilled tickets become negative points at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Be Selective with Tickets
walkthrough.text: Only keep destination tickets you have a realistic plan to complete. On this tight map with just forty trains per player, overcommitting to too many tickets is a recipe for disaster. Look for tickets that overlap with routes you have already claimed or plan to claim. One failed ticket can wipe out the points from a completed one.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have walked through all three action types, seen how tunnels and ferries work, and learned from three common beginner mistakes. Remember to keep tunnel reserves, collect locomotives steadily, and be selective with destination tickets. You are ready to ride the rails across Scandinavia on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Depart
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on route planning and card management.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick definitions of tunnels, ferries, and scoring rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Routes Before You Draw
walkthrough.text: Before you start collecting cards, take a good look at your destination tickets and trace the most efficient path between the cities. Look for routes that serve multiple tickets at once. A single three-car route that helps complete two different tickets is twice as valuable as one that only serves one. Planning your route network before you start drawing cards saves you from collecting colors you do not need.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Train Supply
walkthrough.text: You only have forty trains, and that goes fast on this tight map. Every route you claim eats into your supply, so be strategic about which routes you take. Short routes between key cities are efficient. Long routes score big points but use lots of trains. Count your remaining trains regularly and make sure you have enough to complete your essential ticket connections before the game ends.

walkthrough.title: Tunnel Strategy
walkthrough.text: Tunnels are the defining feature of the Nordic map. The random tunnel tax can cost you zero to three extra cards per attempt. When planning a tunnel claim, always have at least two extra cards of the matching color in hand. If you are short on reserves, consider using locomotives instead, since they are always accepted as extra payment for tunnel costs.

walkthrough.title: Locomotive Management
walkthrough.text: Locomotives are the most versatile cards in the game. They work as wild cards for any color route, they are required for ferry spaces, and they count as extra payment for tunnel costs. But taking one face-up costs your entire draw action. A smart balance is to draw locomotives from the deck when possible and save the face-up grab for moments when you urgently need one and nothing else in the display helps you.

walkthrough.title: Block Strategically
walkthrough.text: With only two or three players on a tight map, blocking is extremely effective. If you notice an opponent collecting lots of one color, look at which routes that color connects and consider claiming one preemptively. Even claiming a short one-car or two-car route that your opponent needs can force them into expensive detours. Just make sure the blocking route also helps your own network.

walkthrough.title: The Globetrotter Race
walkthrough.text: The Globetrotter bonus is worth ten points for completing the most destination tickets. That is a massive swing. If you are going for volume, prioritize short tickets that overlap with your existing network. Each short ticket completed adds to your count for the bonus while requiring minimal extra trains. But be careful not to overextend. A failed ticket costs you points on top of missing the bonus.

walkthrough.title: Watch the End Game
walkthrough.text: Keep an eye on all players' train supplies. When someone gets close to two remaining trains, the end is near. Make sure your critical ticket routes are claimed before the end trigger. There is nothing worse than having cards in hand for a route you needed but running out of time. If you see an opponent running low on trains, consider whether you need to rush your last essential claims before they trigger the end.

walkthrough.title: Card Efficiency
walkthrough.text: The most efficient play is to claim routes that complete multiple tickets with a single set of trains. When choosing which tickets to keep, look for destinations that share common corridors. A network where three tickets all run through a central hub city is far better than three tickets going to three completely different corners of the map. Efficiency in card collection and train placement is the difference between winning and losing.

bullets: Trace routes on the map before collecting cards

Avoid collecting colors you don't need

3 tickets through 1 hub > 3 tickets to 3 corners

walkthrough.title: The Short Ticket Volume Strategy
walkthrough.text: One powerful approach in Nordic Countries is to focus on short destination tickets. Draw tickets frequently and keep only the short ones that overlap with routes you have already claimed or plan to claim. Each completed ticket adds to your Globetrotter count. With ten or more completed tickets, you almost guarantee the ten-point Globetrotter bonus, which alone can decide the game.

walkthrough.title: The Long Route Gambit
walkthrough.text: The northern routes across Scandinavia are some of the highest-scoring routes in the game, with the nine-car route worth twenty-seven points. If you can claim two or three long routes while still completing your tickets, the route points alone can carry you to victory. The downside is that long routes consume many trains and often run through tunnels, adding risk. This strategy works best when your tickets naturally align with the long northern corridor.

walkthrough.title: Aggressive Blocking
walkthrough.text: In a two or three player game, you can see exactly what your opponents are building. If an opponent has a clear north-south corridor developing, claiming one route in the middle of their path can force an expensive detour. The best blocks are routes that also advance your own tickets. Pure spite-blocking wastes your trains, but strategic blocking that serves double duty is devastating.

walkthrough.title: Locomotive Hoarding
walkthrough.text: Locomotives are the most flexible resource in the game. A strategy built around collecting many locomotives gives you the ability to claim any route regardless of color, handle tunnel taxes with ease, and cross ferries without worry. Draw from the deck often to get locomotives at normal draw rate. By mid-game, a hand full of locomotives lets you react to any threat or opportunity on the board.

walkthrough.title: Timing the End Game
walkthrough.text: You control when the game ends by managing your train supply. If you are ahead on tickets and route points, burning through your trains quickly to trigger the end prevents opponents from completing their plans. Conversely, if you need more time, claim shorter routes and draw more cards to stall. Reading the board state and deciding whether to accelerate or delay the end is an advanced skill that separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Network Optimization
walkthrough.text: The strongest players build networks where every route serves at least two purposes. A route that completes one ticket, contributes to a second ticket's path, and blocks an opponent's corridor is a triple-value play. Before claiming a route, always ask yourself: how many tickets does this help complete, does it block anyone, and could I use these trains more efficiently elsewhere? This kind of multi-angle thinking is the hallmark of expert play.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to which face-up cards your opponents take and which routes they claim. If someone takes lots of white cards, look at the white routes on the map and figure out where they are headed. If they claim a route between two unexpected cities, they probably have a ticket connecting that corridor. Use this information to decide whether to block, race for shared routes, or pivot your own strategy to avoid conflict.

walkthrough.title: Adapting to Player Count
walkthrough.text: The game plays very differently at two versus three players. With two players, the map feels spacious and blocking is more targeted. You can afford to plan longer routes and take your time. With three players, the map gets incredibly tight and routes disappear fast. At three, claim essential routes early and be prepared to adapt your ticket strategy if key routes get taken. Flexibility is more important than a perfect plan.

summary.title: The Short Ticket Volume Strategy
bullets: Draw tickets often, keep short overlapping ones
10+ completed tickets → near-guaranteed Globetrotter bonus
Volume approach: many small points + 10 VP bonus

summary.title: The Long Route Gambit
bullets: Northern routes: 9-car = 27 pts, 6-car = 15 pts
High reward but burns trains + tunnel risk
Best when tickets align with long northern corridor

summary.title: Aggressive Blocking
bullets: Claim 1 route in opponent's corridor → forces expensive detour
Best blocks also advance your own tickets (double duty)
Pure spite-blocking wastes your limited trains

summary.title: Locomotive Hoarding
bullets: Locomotives = wild for any color + ferry + tunnel tax
Draw from deck for normal draw rate
Mid-game loco stockpile → react to any opportunity

summary.title: Timing the End Game
bullets: Ahead? Burn trains fast → trigger end before opponents finish
Behind? Claim short routes, draw cards → stall for time
Controlling game pace is an advanced skill

summary.title: Network Optimization
bullets: Every route should serve 2+ purposes (tickets, blocking, scoring)
Triple-value play: complete ticket + contribute to 2nd + block opponent
Ask: how many tickets does this help? Does it block? Better use for trains?

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Track which face-up cards opponents take → infer their routes
Unexpected route claims reveal hidden ticket destinations
Use info to block, race, or pivot your strategy

summary.title: Adapting to Player Count
bullets: 2 players: spacious, targeted blocking, time for long routes
3 players: tight, routes vanish fast, claim essentials early
Flexibility > perfect plan at higher counts

entries.definition: Cards in eight colors plus locomotives used to claim routes on the board. Play a set of matching color cards equal to a route's length to claim it.
Wild card that can substitute for any color when claiming routes. Required for ferry spaces. Counts as matching color for tunnel tax. Taking a face-up locomotive uses your entire draw action.
Card showing two cities and a point value. Complete the connection using your claimed routes for positive points. Fail to connect and lose that many points.
Bonus card worth ten points at game end to the player who completed the most destination tickets. If tied, all tied players receive the bonus.
A colored path of spaces between two adjacent cities on the board. Claim by playing matching cards equal to the route's length. Gray routes accept any single color.

A route marked with a special border. After playing cards, flip three from the draw pile. Each matching color or locomotive revealed costs one extra card. Failing to pay means the attempt fails and cards return to hand.
A route with locomotive icons on some spaces. Those spaces must be filled with locomotive cards specifically. Regular colored cards cannot substitute for locomotive spaces on ferries.
Two parallel routes between the same pair of cities. In Nordic Countries, both routes are available at all player counts.
The numbered track around the board edge. Move your scoring marker forward each time you claim a route to track points during the game.
Points scored immediately when claiming a route. 1 car = 1 pt, 2 cars = 2 pts, 3 cars = 4 pts, 4 cars = 7 pts, 5 cars = 10 pts, 6 cars = 15 pts, 9 cars = 27 pts.
Five train car cards placed face up beside the draw pile. Players may draw from these or the deck. Refill to five after each card taken.
The face-down deck of train car cards. Draw blind from the top when the face-up display does not have what you need. Shuffle the discard pile to form a new draw pile when empty.
A route or city with limited alternative paths. Blocking a chokepoint forces opponents into long detours using extra trains.
When any player has two or fewer trains remaining after their turn, every player gets one final turn before scoring.
Forty plastic train pieces per player. Place on the board when claiming routes. When your supply runs low, the end game approaches.
A route with no specific color. Can be claimed using any single color of matching cards. You cannot mix colors to claim a gray route.
The additional cards that may be required when claiming a tunnel. Zero to three extra cards per attempt, determined by three random card flips.
The connected set of routes a player has claimed. Destination tickets require a continuous network path between their two cities.
Destination tickets that share common routes in their optimal paths. Keeping overlapping tickets is efficient because one route helps complete multiple goals.
